risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The parent study was designed as a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial... 451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups" Comment: The paper states that participants were randomized/randomly assigned, but it does not describe how the allocation sequence was generated (e.g., computer-generated list, random number table, block randomization, stratification). Under Cochrane criteria, 'randomized' alone without further method detail is insufficient to confirm a truly random sequence, and there is no evidence of a clearly non-random method either.

Quote: "451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups" and "The parent study was designed as a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial." Comment: The report confirms random assignment, but provides no explicit information on how the allocation was concealed from the person enrolling participants (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy-controlled allocation, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes). Because the method for preventing foreknowledge of upcoming assignments is not described, allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The parent study was designed as a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial... 451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups: (1) dispersible Zn tablet... (2) liquid Zn supplement... or (3) liquid placebo supplement." Comment: The trial explicitly states it was only "partially-masked," indicating blinding of participants/personnel was incomplete. Because the intervention formats differed visibly (dispersible tablet vs liquid supplement vs liquid placebo), knowledge of assignment by participants/caregivers and study personnel is plausible. The study also included outcomes that could be influenced by behavior or co-interventions if allocation was known (e.g. morbidity), so incomplete blinding creates a credible risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "The parent study was designed as a randomized, partially-masked, placebo-controlled intervention trial" and "The major outcome variable was the change in plasma zinc concentration following three weeks of zinc supplementation"; outcomes were measured using laboratory/standardized systems: "Plasma zinc concentrations were analyzed by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrophotometry," "Plasma CRP and AGP concentrations were measured by a combined sandwich ELISA," "Plasma HRP2 concentrations... were measured colorimetrically," and "Weight and length were measured following standard techniques." Comment: The outcome assessors were primarily lab/imaging systems and standardized measurement procedures rather than patient self-report or clinician judgement. Although the report does not explicitly state that outcome assessors were blinded, the reported outcomes are objective biochemical and anthropometric measures, and there are no subjective self-reported or clinician-rated outcomes in this study report. Therefore, knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced outcome measurement.

Quote: "451 eligible study participants were randomly assigned to 1 of 3 treatment groups... After the baseline assessment period, all children received a daily dose of supplement for 21 days" and "Of 451 participants enrolled in the main zinc supplementation study, the L:M test was completed successfully in 282 (63%)... Predominant reasons for unsuccessful tests were incomplete consumption of test sugars (N = 70, 16%) and leakage or fecal contamination of urine samples (N = 67, 15%)." Comment: The only reported losses/exclusions are for the baseline L:M sub-study test, which was conducted "prior to the start of zinc supplementation," so these do not establish post-randomization attrition on the review outcomes of interest (death, diarrhoea, LRTI, malaria, vomiting). The paper does not report per-arm numbers randomized vs analysed at follow-up for those outcomes, nor post-randomization missing outcome data/reasons by arm. Therefore attrition bias for the outcomes of interest cannot be judged confidently from this report.
